section: High Temperature Oxidation of Iron-Manganese-Aluminum Based Alloys

context_update.summary: This chunk introduces the study, describing the Fe–Mn–Al ternary alloy system with Mn 0-40% and Al 0-15%, oxidized at 600, 800, and 1000°C in 200 torr O2 up to 100 h. Specimens are as-cast discs (~7 mm dia, 1 mm thick) from levitation melting. Kinetics are recorded continuously for 24 h at 800°C, but few alloys obey simple rate laws; therefore, 24 h weight gains (ΔW/24) are compared. The effects of Mn and Al on weight gain are presented: <2% Mn increases rate, 5-10% Mn reduces it; minima occur at 6-9% Al. Four distinct scale morphologies are identified and mapped onto the 800°C isothermal phase diagram as an oxide map (Fig. 3). Regions: I (low Al) — large Fe-oxide nodules with inner Fe3O4 and outer Fe2O3, Mn/Al enrichment, transition from large to small nodules at ~7.5% Mn; II — bulky Mn-rich oxides linearly above austenite, stratified scales, wormlike Mn oxide threads, spalled alumina; III — protective adherent alumina with small protrusions, very low weight gains; IV (>9% Al) — alumina penetrated by small Mn-oxide nodules even at <5% Mn. The most resistant alloys are fully ferritic Fe–(5–10)Mn–(6–10)Al. Austenite promotes breakdown, and chromium additions reduce critical Al. Key figures: 1–13, Table I. No sample aliases yet assigned.
material_systems: Specific compositions: Fe–7.5Mn–5Al, Fe–10Mn–5Al, Fe–15Mn–6Al, Fe–20Mn–6Al
processing_route: Levitation melting of high-purity materials
As-cast disc, ~7 mm diameter, 1 mm thickness
Isothermal oxidation at 600, 800, 1000°C in static pure O2 at 200 torr
Oxidation times: 24 h (routine), selected alloys up to 100 h
Continuous weight recording with Cahn R.G. electrobalance at 800°C
Quenching after oxidation for phase verification
microstructure: Four scale morphologies mapped on 800°C oxide map: Region I (alumina + large/small Fe-oxide nodules), Region II (bulky Mn-rich oxides related to austenite), Region III (protective alumina with minor protrusions), Region IV (alumina penetrated by Mn-oxide nodules)
Large nodules: Fe2O3 outer, Fe3O4 inner; Mn and Al enrichment in Fe3O4 and at gas-oxide interface
Small nodules: Mn-oxide outer layer, Fe2O3 beneath, Al-rich layer at alloy interface
Stratified scales above austenite-depleted ferrite layers; wormlike Mn oxide threads
Fe2O3 disappears above 20% Mn
Spalled alumina associated with voids and bulky Mn-rich oxides
properties: Weight gain after 24 h (ΔW/24) used for comparison due to non-parabolic kinetics
Minima in weight gain at 6-9% Al
Mn additions: <2% increase rate, 5-10% decrease rate
Region III shows very low weight gains and protective behavior
mechanisms: Alumina scale protection requires sufficient Al; alloy phase (austenite) disrupts protective scales
Nodule growth through alumina scales even at high Al contents (Region IV)
Austenite-induced breakdown results in bulky Mn-rich oxides
Chromium lowers critical Al content for alumina formation
figures_or_tables: Fig. 1: Log-log weight gain vs time at 800°C
Fig. 2: Effect of Mn and Al on 24 h weight gain
Fig. 3: Oxide map on 800°C phase diagram
Fig. 4: Cross-section of large nodule on Fe-7.5Mn-5Al
Fig. 5: X-ray maps of Fe, Mn, Al in nodule on Fe-7.5Mn-5Al
Fig. 6: Large and small nodules on Fe-7.5Mn-5Al surface
Fig. 7: Cross-section of small nodule on Fe-10Mn-5Al
Fig. 8: Bulky Mn-rich oxide above austenite on Fe-15Mn-6Al
Fig. 9: Stratified oxide above ferrite layer on Fe-20Mn-6Al
Fig. 11: Spalled alumina with voids and bulky oxides
Fig. 12: Small protrusions on Region III alloys
unresolved_terms: Specific phase identities of Mn oxides (only described as manganese-rich or grey layer)
Quantitative composition of the 'thin dark aluminum-rich oxide layer' at nodule-alloy interface

context_update.summary: This chunk provides details on the oxide map Region IV (Al > 9%), where alumina scales are penetrated by Mn-rich oxide nodules with a thin Al-rich layer at the interface, and extreme Mn enrichment even below 5% Mn. It reports 100 h oxidation data for Fe–10Mn–6Al and Fe–20Mn–8Al at 600, 800, and 1000°C (Table II). Fe–10Mn–6Al shows alumina with bulky/threadlike oxides at 600°C, scale lifting/buckling at 800°C (Fig. 14a), and thick convoluted two-layer alumina at 1000°C (Fig. 14b). Fe–20Mn–8Al exhibits fewer bulky oxides with severe spalling at 800°C and spalled convoluted alumina at 1000°C, with an external layer enriched in Mn/Al and an inner layer low in Fe/Mn. The section then introduces chromium-containing Fe–Mn–Al–Cr alloys, showing that Cr improves resistance. Alloys with <5% Al suffer breakaway unless Cr is high (e.g., Fe–10Mn–4Al–12Cr); ~15% Mn also causes breakaway. After 24 h, only Fe–10Mn–4Al–12Cr, Fe–5Mn–5Al–5Cr and Fe–7.5Mn–5Al–5Cr form protective alumina. After 100 h, all degrade; low-Al alloys form hollow convoluted mounds (Fig. 15a) with tiny Mn-rich nodules and threads (Fig. 15b). Spallation leaves voids and internal oxides. Duplex Fe–10Mn–4Al–5Cr replicates region II scales over austenite, while Fe–15Mn–5Al–5Cr forms detached highly convoluted scales on fully ferritic Mn-depleted substrate (Fig. 16). The chunk includes Fig. 10–16 and Tables II–III, which will be needed for further discussion of scale evolution and the role of Cr.
processing_route: Isothermal oxidation at 600, 800, 1000°C in static pure O2 at 200 torr
Oxidation time 100 h for Fe-10Mn-6Al and Fe-20Mn-8Al
Oxidation times 24 h and 100 h for Fe-Mn-Al-Cr alloys at 800°C
Note: Fe–5Mn–4Al–5Cr reported ΔW/24 actually corresponds to 15 h oxidation
microstructure: Region IV: alumina scales penetrated by Mn-oxide nodules with thin Al-rich layer at interface; extreme Mn enrichment even at <5% Mn
Fe-10Mn-6Al 600°C/100h: alumina + bulky/threadlike oxides
Fe-10Mn-6Al 800°C/100h: scale lifting, buckling, small bulky oxides
Fe-10Mn-6Al 1000°C/100h: thick convoluted two-layer alumina, edge detachment
Fe-20Mn-8Al 800°C/100h: fewer bulky oxides, severe spallation
Fe-20Mn-8Al 1000°C/100h: convoluted alumina, external Mn/Al enrichment, inner low Fe/Mn, spalled on cooling
Cr-containing alloys <5% Al: hollow convoluted/wrinkled oxide mounds, tiny Mn-rich nodules, occasional Mn-oxide threads, spalled alumina, substrate voids, internal oxides
Duplex Fe-10Mn-4Al-5Cr: alumina + bulky oxides over precipitated austenite (region II type)
Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr: detached highly convoluted scales, fully ferritic Mn-depleted substrate, Al-rich scale base, thicker sections highly Mn-enriched
properties: 100 h weight gains (Table II): Fe-10Mn-6Al 0.171 (600°C), 0.117 (800°C), 0.450 (1000°C) mg/cm²; Fe-20Mn-8Al 0.101 (600°C), 0.242* (800°C), 0.253* (1000°C) (* unreliable due to spalling)
Cr-containing alloy weight gains (Table III, 800°C): protective 24 h gains ≤0.068 mg/cm² (Fe-5Mn-5Al-5Cr, Fe-7.5Mn-5Al-5Cr); breakaway identified for <5% Al or ~15% Mn
mechanisms: Cr improves oxidation resistance but protective behavior requires sufficient Al and limited Mn
Al <5% or Mn ~15% leads to breakaway
Spallation and scale lifting indicate degradation of protective alumina at extended times or higher temperatures
Nodule penetration in region IV and internal oxidation under spalled regions indicate outward Mn diffusion and inward oxygen transport

context_update.summary: Current chunk first reports the oxidation scale on Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr after 24 h at 800°C: extremely convoluted and detached scale, totally ferritic Mn-depleted substrate, Al enrichment at scale base, Mn highly enriched in thicker sections (Fig. 16). The Discussion then explains how Al additions to Fe-Mn alloys prevent (Fe,Mn)O by forming low-dissociation-pressure Al2O3 or aluminate spinels at the interface. It justifies the 800°C oxide map (Fig. 3) by assuming equilibrium-like phases after 24 h, supported by small structural changes in Fe-10Mn-6Al and Fe-20Mn-8Al after 100 h. The chunk details Region I: alloys with insufficient Al form alumina interspersed with bulky nodules. Nodules on <7.5% Mn are large with outer Fe2O3 and inner (Fe,Mn)3O4; on >7.5% Mn they are small with continuous outer Mn2O3 and healing aluminate/alumina layers at the base (Fig. 17). Fe-7.5Mn-5Al marks the transition. Mechanisms are proposed: partial isolation of (Fe,Mn)3O4 by alumina/aluminate enhances Fe2O3 growth; complete isolation stops nodule growth. Continuous Mn2O3 retards diffusion, enabling healing layers. Minima in weight gain at 5-10% Mn are linked to replacement of large Fe-rich nodules by small Mn-rich ones. Evidence includes Fig. 4 (Mn2O3 particles in Fe2O3), Fig. 17 schematics, and references to Fig. 2a. This chunk consolidates the understanding of Region I oxide structures and their evolution, providing key mechanistic explanations that subsequent sections may build upon or contrast with other regions.
material_systems: Fe-Mn-Al ternary alloys in Region I
processing_route: Isothermal oxidation at 800°C for 24 h
Isothermal oxidation at 800°C for 100 h for selected alloys
key_variables: Presence and continuity of healing layers (Al2O3, aluminate)
microstructure: Large nodules: outer Fe2O3, inner (Fe,Mn)3O4, Al2O3/aluminate particles at interface
Small nodules: outer continuous Mn2O3, inner Fe2O3/(Fe,Mn)3O4, continuous healing Al2O3/aluminate layer at base
Type 1, 2, 3 nodules as in Fig. 17
Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr: fully ferritic substrate, Mn depletion, Al enrichment at scale base
Grey Mn2O3 particles in Fe2O3 scale on Fe-7.5Mn-5Al
properties: Weight gain minima at 5-10% Mn (Fig. 2a) due to replacement of large iron oxide nodules with small Mn-rich nodules
mechanisms: Al prevents (Fe,Mn)O via low dissociation pressure of Al2O3/spinels
Partial isolation of (Fe,Mn)3O4 by alumina/aluminate increases Fe2O3 growth ratio
Complete isolation suppresses (Fe,Mn)3O4 and yields Fe2O3-dominant nodules
Continuous outer Mn2O3 retards ionic diffusion, allowing healing layer formation
Intermittent breakaway and healing create irregular interfaces

context_update.summary: This chunk continues the discussion of Region II of the 800°C oxide map for Fe–Mn–Al alloys, detailing the oxidation behaviour of duplex (α+γ) and one triplex (α+γ+βMn) alloy (Fe–30Mn–9Al). It describes two scale morphologies depending on the ferrite/austenite ratio: high ferrite leads to alumina with bulky Mn-rich oxides and oxide threads on grain boundaries, while high austenite yields bulky Mn-rich oxides with wormlike threads growing through alumina. The poor oxidation resistance arises from austenite’s low Al content (max 7–8%), which cannot support protective alumina. The true protective/non-protective boundary above ~12% Mn follows the α/(α+γ) phase boundary. A five-stage growth model for bulky oxides on duplex alloys is presented in Fig. 18: (1) alumina on ferrite, (2) austenite nucleation, (3) Mn2O3 threads at weak spots, (4) incorporation into bulky oxide with Mn depletion, (5) healing Al2O3/aluminate. Oxide structures depend on Mn: <20% Mn resemble region I small nodules, >20% Mn are Fe2O3‑free Mn-rich phases. Weight gains increase with Mn and decrease with Al; breakaway/healing behaviours are linked to aluminate formation. Long-term (100 h) tests on Fe–10Mn–6Al and Fe–20Mn–8Al from 600–1000°C show stable protective boundaries at 800°C, need for higher Al at 600°C, and protective convoluted alumina scales at 1000°C due to phase boundary shifts. 1000°C scales on Fe–20Mn–8Al spall and consist of inner Al2O3 and outer Mn3O4–Al2O3 solid solution with >30 wt% Al2O3. Key figures include Figs. 8a, 9, 10a, 10b, 17, 18, and 19. Subsequent chunks may address further regions or discuss chromium-alloyed variants.

Oxidation times: 24 h and 100 h
Oxygen pressure: 200 torr
Levitation-melted, as‑cast discs, ~7 mm × 1 mm
microstructure: Duplex α+γ and triplex α+γ+βMn structures
Alumina scales with bulky Mn-rich oxides and oxide threads (ferrite‑dominant)
Bulky Mn-rich oxides with wormlike threads through alumina (austenite‑dominant)
Secondary ferrite layers formed by Mn depletion
Five‑stage growth model for bulky oxides (Fig. 18)
Structures of bulky oxides: <20% Mn similar to region I small nodules, >20% Mn Fe2O3‑free Mn-rich phases
Scale on Fe–25Mn–7Al: Mn2O3, Mn3O4, (Fe,Mn)3O4, and (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 or Al2O3 (Fig. 19)
Alumina/aluminate healing layers
Mn3O4–Al2O3 solid solution outer layer at 1000°C (>30 wt% Al2O3)
properties: Poor oxidation resistance of duplex region II alloys
Weight gains increase with Mn and decrease with Al; rapid increases >10% Mn
Fe–20Mn–6Al: >1 mg weight gain after 20 h at 800°C
Austenitic alloy resistance ~1 mg/day attributed to healing layers
100 h tests: Fe–10Mn–6Al and Fe–20Mn–8Al show stable boundary at 800°C, non‑protective at 600°C, protective at 1000°C
mechanisms: Austenite causes breakdown because of insufficient Al (≤7–8%)
Protective/non‑protective boundary follows α/(α+γ) boundary above ~12% Mn
Secondary ferrite oxidation resistance depends on Mn level: <30% Mn leads to region I (non‑protective), >30% Mn enables region III but with β‑Mn
Growth model: alumina film → austenite nucleation → Mn2O3 threads → bulky oxide incorporation → healing layer
Healing kinetics attributed to alumina/aluminate formation reducing cation outward diffusion
Mn enrichment and (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 growth control Mn3O4 and (Fe,Mn)3O4 stability; continuous aluminate layer removes (Fe,Mn)3O4
Temperature‑dependent phase boundary shift: α/(α+γ) moves to lower Al with increasing T^

context_update.summary: This chunk covers Region II residual (Fe-20Mn-8Al at 1000°C spalls with inner Al2O3 and outer Mn3O4-Al2O3 solid solution >30% Al2O3). Region III is fully ferritic with adherent protective alumina, extremely low 24h weight gains (~0.05 mg/cm²) for <15% Mn, optimum Mn 5-10%. Upper boundary marks continuous vs. nodule-penetrated alumina; lower boundary shows Mn reduces Al required for external alumina, linked to preferential Mn oxidation. Region IV features alumina with small Mn-rich nodules; high-Al alloys spall leaving high-density smooth rounded voids (also for >25% Mn); Fe-30Mn-12Al shows Mn-oxide-filled voids (Fig. 11c). A six-stage nodule growth mechanism (Fig. 20) involves Mn vapor nucleation, outward Mn²⁺ diffusion (p-type oxides), inward oxygen short-circuit filling voids with Mn oxide, and healing layer formation. Cr-containing alloys: Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr convoluted scales (Fig. 16a), Fe-10Mn-4Al-5Cr bulky Mn-rich oxides, Fe-7.5Mn-4Al-5Cr oxide threads due to Al-depletion-induced austenite. Conclusions: Fully ferritic Fe-(5-10)%Mn-(6-10)%Al highly resistant; Cr reduces Al requirement; avoid austenite. Key figures: Fig. 20, Fig. 11a-c, Fig. 3, Fig. 2b, Fig. 16a, Fig. 15b.

Isothermal oxidation at 600, 800, 1000°C in 200 torr O2
24 h oxidation routine, selected 100 h tests
Quenching after oxidation for phase evaluation

Region IV: alumina with small Mn-rich nodules and smooth rounded voids
Fe-30Mn-12Al: voids filled with Mn oxide
Convoluted scales on Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr, uneven thickness
Bulky Mn-rich oxides above austenite on Fe-10Mn-4Al-5Cr
Oxide threads on Fe-7.5Mn-4Al-5Cr after 100 h
Healing layers Al2O3 or (Fe,Mn)Al2O4
properties: Extremely low 24h weight gain ~0.05 mg/cm² (Region III <15% Mn)
Poor cyclic oxidation of 20-30% Mn alloys due to scale spallation
mechanisms: Preferential Mn oxidation reduces Al requirement for external alumina
Small Mn nodule growth: nucleation over voids, Mn vapor, outward Mn2+ diffusion, inward short-circuit O2 filling void with Mn oxide, healing layer (Fig. 20)
Void formation related to Al concentration
Austenite facilitates breakdown of pre-existing alumina scales
